speaker: interviewer
text: Gerda Frieberg nee Steinitz. We were talking about your parents hiding in the cemetery, and they were discovered by the Judenrat. What happened after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: When they were discovered by the Jewish police, my mother asked them if they would take them home first, because they were soaked. It was raining. And she'd like to change. And so they agreed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they went back to her room-- at the time, she was already living somewhere else-- and when she came, they walked in with her into the room, and she says, would you mind waiting outside? I only have a room, one room, and I cannot undress when you are present. And they said, OK. And so they walked out of the room.

speaker: interviewee
text: She asked my father to move the coat hangers in the cupboard while she opened the window. And they both jumped out while the guys were standing behind the door. So when they entered, they both were gone. And so they ran away to a neighboring town. But that town was already empty of Jews, and they had nowhere to go. They had nowhere to stay. And so my-- they were-- my father was discovered, and they took him away. He was taken by the Nazis, and he was terribly beaten up. She saw him in the police station. But they send him away to a concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the name of this town?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was in [? Modrzejow ?]. And so my mother was left alone. She knew where we were.

speaker: interviewer
text: She had received you letter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I found other ways. I don't give up. I never did. I never will. And so she was terribly lonely, scared, alone. She knew where we were. There's no point in her hanging around, sleeping under the stairs in hallways. She knew my story because I, through somebody, I send a letter. I got smarter after what happened. And she knew my story, how I got to my sister's camp. So she went, took a few things together, her parents' photos, in a little suitcase that my father had from 1920-- 1914. And she went to Sosnowiec, to the same holding camp, to the [NON-ENGLISH], where I was held, asked the guard to let her in, and walked down straight to the office of the commander, and said, I would like you-- I came here on my own will, and I would ask you to send me to be with my children. They are in concentration camp in Ober Altstadt in Sudetenland.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, just a moment. When was it? She said, about a year ago. Oh, he says, I remember. He took a Nazi guard, an SS guard, put her on a train, and sent her to Ober Altstadt. We, of course, not knowing anything where our parents are, thought that all, that they were all deported, because these rumors came back, because the young people were sent to work, and the old people were, went right to the gas chambers. So we knew what happened to the ghettos, not knowing was our parents with the young or the old.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were getting ready to go to camp on a Saturday afternoon after work, when we were all lined up-- and we were always being counted, always being counted-- and I see on the bridge across, an SS guard with a woman with a little suitcase. And as they came closer, I cried out to my sister, Hannah, this is our mother. And this was the last time I called her mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was the only mother in camp, and I could not put-- how many were we then? About 800 by then. The camp grew to 800. I could not have 800 girls hear me call my mother when they did not have a mother. And so that's how we were reunited. February, '42, April, '42, April, '43. We still don't know where my father is. One night we walk after the night shift from work to the camp. A man is going by bike up and down, up and down again. I come to camp. A girl comes over, and she says, Gerda, I have a letter for you. A letter? Did you see the man on the bicycle? Yeah. He says, whenever the guard wasn't near, he says, are the Steinitz girls in this camp? And so I said, yes. I have a letter from their father.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father sent this man from that factory where he worked, in [NON-ENGLISH], which was not very far from our camp. He wanted us to know that he is alive. And we were getting mail, now and then, and we were writing to him the same way. We knew that he was alive. He knew that we are alive. And the last birthday gift that I got was in 1944, in October, when he had somebody to make a little signature ring from metal in the factory machine shop. And he sent in that letter the little ring for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you still have that ring?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm hmm. I have that ring. That's a priceless possession. And so, we at least knew he was alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: To get back to my question, you were talking about you working in the factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were working with men?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Local Czech, German laborers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Not only this, but in the same factory, we had prisoners of war that were volunteers from South Africa working in the same factory. And they were delivering machine parts. And they would do all kind of work. They would come into machine shop.

speaker: interviewee
text: One in particular took interest in me. And he would drop a note, and I would very quickly step on that note. And he would ask me if we need anything, and how can they help? I was the link between the camp and the prisoners of war. And I would tell him that we don't have medicine, that we need-- that we have typhoid fever in camp, and that we need cigarettes in order to buy medication to-- that we need tea to stop the diarrhea.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were receiving Red Cross parcels. So I would drop something when he walked by. And this is how we communicated. Of course, if I would have been caught, I wouldn't be here telling you this story. The first three that was in our camp, I would be hung in the presence of everyone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other women hung in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: They removed them. And we never knew what happened to them until after the war, when I found a mass grave. One day-- and he was leaving things for me in the blacksmith's shop, because on Saturday, I was the only one that worked. And he volunteered to work on Saturday. And I would go in there, and we had a space where he hid it, and I would take it. And so I would have some cigarettes to get medicine. I was able to save the life of my very best friend, who was dying. Dr. Mengele came to our camp to inspect. He went into the sick room, and he said-- saw the people there, and he saw my friend-- and he says, next time I come, I don't want to see her anymore. Get rid of her. She's taking up space, and she's eating food.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would they do with people who had gone beyond--

speaker: interviewee
text: They disappeared.

speaker: interviewer
text: --medicine?

speaker: interviewee
text: We don't know. So I practically saved her life by getting her the black tea to stop her diarrhea, to exchange cigarettes for medication. Germans didn't have cigarettes. The prisoners got it from outside, from Switzerland. But this was all in risking my life doing it. But the most precious thing that he left for me was a map of Europe. And I took it, and I hid it in camp. And every so often, he would drop me a little note where the armies were. Now if that they would have caught, I didn't need a judge and jury. My fate would have been sealed. And I would make marks on the map. We knew exactly where the, where the armies were. And they were giving us encouragement. They had a clandestine radio in their camp. And so we knew. Well, later on we didn't need even them to tell us that the airplanes that were bombing Dresden, Magdeburg, and so on, were flying over our camps in the broad daylight.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wanted to ask you, in this camp, you were only women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were local men laborers and there were prisoners of war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any opportunity for the men and the women to fraternize?

speaker: interviewee
text: The man were not-- first of all, they were all German or Czech. Some were Polish workers, some were Ukrainians. We were-- there were guards all around us. We were not-- we were only allowed to speak to the foreman regarding our work. If we needed more material or if we needed some oil to oil the machine or any tool or anything, we could talk to the foreman, but not to the people. Even when we worked with another woman at the machine, when there were machines that two people worked, we are not allowed to-- some Czech women took pity on us, and they would bring-- remember we were there three years already. You don't become a stranger. It's not that they brought us in one day and the next day we moved on. This was, this was my luck. [INAUDIBLE] I was taken to camp so early when most young people were still home. Where people in Hungary lived a normal life, if you can call it normal, I was already in camp. But then I was spared to go to Auschwitz, or be shift from one camp to another. So this is what was, you know, the good part of it. The Czech women would sometimes leave some food, piece of bread, for us in a corner somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as far as you know, you were not aware of any sexual fraternization--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: --between the camp inmates--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and the German soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all. We lived in constant fear of being raped by the guards, because apparently there were some cases. I only heard about it after the war because I was considered-- first of all, German again, among the Polish Jewish girls. Secondly, I was considered child, stupid. You don't tell things like this. I found out after the war that a baby was born in our camp, and I didn't know that.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's what I was leading up to ask you.

speaker: interviewee
text: A woman came, very young woman, came-- married, was separated from her husband. When she came, nobody could tell she was pregnant. And the baby was born in our camp. And we had a nurse there that was also a prisoner, delivered the baby and smothered it. But I didn't know that while I was in camp. I found out after the war. I wasn't told these things. I was, number one, too young to--

speaker: interviewer
text: With your permission, I'd like to ask you another personal question. Many of the labor camps, the women inmates said that their menstruation stopped.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm hmm, mine. I could have had a baby. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this the case--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --at Ober Altstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard-- it was strange. The menstruation stopped, and we wondered how this is possible. Not all, some. I heard after the war, much later, that they put something in our food. I don't know if this is possible. I don't know if it is true. But it might have been malnutrition. Your whole system stopped working. You got so little food that the whole system stopped working. And many times, they deprived you of the food that you were supposed to get, no matter what happened. We had a case where a girl ran away. She ran away. Somehow she made, communicated with some Czech person, and they promised to hide her. And she disappeared. And so we were all-- when we came back from work, it was in, early in February. We were all lined up on, on-- there was like a square in that camp, where we always were counted. In the morning when we left, we were counted. When we came back, we were counted. This was an obsession with them. Before we got our food, we were counted. We had a girl that had epilepsy. And when she got an attack, we would hold her up, so they wouldn't know, because she would have disappeared the same day. And whenever she got an attack, we would-- two girls on both sides would hold her up, so they could count the heads.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we came back from work, and they made us line up. And they were counting. And one was missing. And then they wanted to know where she is, and they thought we know about it, that it's a conspiracy. We did not get our food that evening. And we had to stand there all night. We stood all night, because they told us that if they don't find her-- the commander, the SS commander came out. And by the way, he was the right hand of the commandant of [? Placzkow ?]. He was sent from [? Placzkow ?] after the Soviets have liberated the camp. These SS guards were sent deeper into Germany, so they wouldn't be out of work. What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name-- he was an unterscharfuhrer of the SS, and his name was Franz [NON-ENGLISH], and he ran our camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was transferred to your camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: To our-- from [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1943-- late-- no, it was already '44. In 1944. Yeah. You know, when you were at a camp, you didn't know dates, time. Every day was the same. You just know the sun went down, so the day was finished. And what we didn't know in camp is when our holidays came. We always counted by the moon. There were some very Orthodox girls in our camp, and they made sure that we had a calendar. And they made sure that we knew when the different holidays came. We would go down when the SS went to sleep, and the lights went out. We went down from our bunk beds, and whoever remembered the prayers, we would have a little ceremony, a little service. And then we finish by whispering the Hatikvah. Until today, when I hear the Hatikvah, I see myself standing in the dark, and singing it in a whisper. Until today. I see people sing, and I cry, over 50 years later. That's what it means to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we only knew for Jewish holidays when Passover came. An Orthodox girl would start bartering. They would give bread, and take our potatoes. They would not touch bread. And they never ate the meat. Even that little food that we got, they would give you some piece of the meat for a piece of bread. So there we were, standing all night, and he came out, and he said, tomorrow morning, if we don't find her-- we're now looking for her-- I will deal with it. The morning came. Remember, this is February. We don't have a coat. We don't have shoes. We just have our feet wrapped around in straw. We'd take the flax from the factory. We had no-- we did not get food that evening. He comes. He's going to choose 10 girls, and every hour, he would shoot one till they find the one that disappeared. And among the 10 was my sister and I. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So there we were. We were-- by now there were 1,000 women in the camp already. And the 10 of us stand. And he walks in front of us, and he says, how old are you? 18. Well, he says, it's nice to be 18, but you'll never live to be 19, because you're going to be the second to be shot. You have about two hours to live.

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, I don't know what happened. I only know that the woman commander came, and I think they had some kind of a romance going there, the two of them. There were were the rumors in camp. I was too young. I was a child growing up. I never had a chance to grow up and mature. I might have been 18, but I was still 14. But there were rumors that there was something going on between them, and she spoke to him. And then she left. And he said, OK. I let everybody go. And the 10 of you stay here. So now we think that he is going to shoot us all at once. Can't even tell you what were the thoughts we had. I only thought of my mother. I was glad he sent everybody into the barracks, that she wouldn't witness it. [SOBBING] It was better to die then than later. It didn't matter. Then he suddenly said, I'm going to count to 10. And when I count to 10, I don't see any one of you out here. You can run to the barrack. Anyone who's left outside is shot. He took out the pistol. And he makes it ready, whatever you do with a pistol. And he starts counting. And we start running on that ice and on that snow, and trying to get on the stairs into the barrack and pushing in through that narrow door. You only think of yourself. I want to live, not my friend, not the girl behind me or in front of me. I want to be through that door and save my life. It's something you'd never do-- enormous circumstances. What I'm trying to say is, this is the way they dehumanized us, that we didn't care about our fellow prisoners. That suddenly, in that moment, you cared about yourself. That explains it all. That explains how people behaved in a time like this. How many people are being accused of working with the Nazis or for the Nazis. But if a man had a family at home and thought that this will save his family, in a time like this, you've got to be in his shoes, and you can never be in his shoes unless you were there. And you can never sit in judgment. And that moment when I tried to get through that door before any of my fellow prisoners, I understood what they did to us. These were the episodes in camp. And there were many of them like this. There were times when we were standing all night, and in the morning, the guard would come, and the guys would kiss the, the, the girls there in front of us. Don't know what they had in mind. Didn't bother me. I didn't know what I was supposed to feel when they did it, but there were older girls. They were married women there, young women. And they would take out a sandwich and take one bite, throw it in the mud, and stamp it out with their feet. We would kill for a piece of bread. The orders did not come from Berlin to do this. So I cannot buy the excuse that the Nazis worked on orders from the authorities. These were individuals' decisions. They did not need to do this. We lived, if you can call it life, with the hope and with a prayer. We could not give up faith. We had nothing else left. And we couldn't give, give up hope, for no one can live without hope. We saw outside the camp the young people walking hand in hand, boy and girl, families walking with their children, and thinking of where are our families. I was lucky. I had a sister. I had a mother. My friends were not that lucky. In the whole camp, there were seven pairs of sisters among 1,000 women and one mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you remain there at Ober Altstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was, I was three years in camp. The last days we were taken-- the factory had no raw material anymore, and we couldn't work anymore. And they weren't getting-- they're not going to feed us if we don't work. So they took us out, marched us outside the town to dig trenches, that when the tanks roll in-- because we could already hear the-- we could hear already the guns, day and night. We knew it's over. And the planes were flying over. And they would herd us into one room, because they said that we would give them signals to bomb the factories. See, and you were always afraid that they will accuse you of it. You always lived in fear. You lived in fear of the men who would, who would beat you. You see, women had different experiences in camp. Women were beaten by men. Women were afraid to be raped. Women were in camp, the young married women, mothers, the children were torn away from them. And there they saw German children walking with their families. Women had been put to work in places where men couldn't work, that it was too difficult for men. It was a very different experience to be a woman prisoner than it was a man prisoner. I'm not minimizing the men's experiences, but there was a difference.

speaker: interviewer
text: No doubt.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were taken to dig the, the trenches so then the tanks come that they should at least hold them back, because, of course, the Germans didn't even have anything anymore to keep them back. And we were working, and it was already May. And it was raining. And it's in the mountains. And it's cold. And we only have that one coat, and it's soaked. And there is no where to dry the wet clothes. You put it on in the morning again, as you took it off in the evening. And you're marching in a column there, and they give you the shovel, and you are-- they're walking with the dogs, and you don't dare to even look up. And one day, we hear the planes overhead. And we look up. It looks like it's snowing. And as it comes closer down, we realized these were flyers, pieces of paper. Immediately, the orders from the guards that if you dared to pick one up, they shoot you. Well, they cannot control 1,000 women and all the paper that was come down. As one piece of paper came close by me, I put a shovel of dirt on top of it and continued working. And when I picked it up later and took it to camp and read it, it was a message for us. Hold on. We are coming to liberate you. And I can only think of the pilot who flew over enemy territory to give us the greatest gift of all. He gave us hope. There was no hope, but he gave us hope. Hold on. I don't know if he returned safely home. But I'll always be grateful. On May 9, 8, they called us out again for an assembly. And we got a whole speech that, yes, the war is coming to an end. And yes, the allied are winning the war, but that doesn't mean that we will be free, because the camp is rigged with dynamite, and they will blow us up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this the commandant?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And the camp is being blown up before the allied marched, march in. So we should not celebrate yet. As a matter of fact, again, as I said, Jewish humor, in the most difficult time, the woman commandant said, well, even if you manage to stay alive, if the Russians come, they will rape you. And one of the girls said, so when are they coming? [LAUGHS] This is, this is what you needed to survive that moment.
